title: Activate Your King
summary: In the endgame, your king fights

steps.title: King Power
steps.text:
## The endgame king

In the **middlegame** the king is a fugitive — castled, hidden, defended by his pawns. In the **endgame**, with most pieces traded off, that timidity becomes a luxury you cannot afford. The king is now a **fighting piece**, roughly worth a strong minor piece, and every winning plan in a simple ending starts with him.

### What this position is

- White king on **e5**, already escorting his pawn from in front.
- Pawn on **e4**, two squares from the queening lane.
- Black king on **e8**, blocking the path.

This is the **K + P vs K** archetype — the most common winning structure in chess.

### The procedure to convert

1. **Lead with the king**, never the pawn. The pawn pushes only when the king has cleared the way.
2. **Outflank** the enemy king by stepping diagonally forward: {{Kd6}} or {{Kf6}}.
3. **Take the opposition** — when kings face each other one square apart and your opponent must move, you control the dance.
4. **Then push the pawn** to claim more space and force a queen.

### Common mistakes

- Pushing {{e5}} too early — the pawn arrives without escort and is blockaded.
- Stepping back with the king (e.g. Ke4) — you give up tempo and the opposition.
- Wandering the king sideways without any plan to outflank.

### A model line

{{Kd6}} ... Kd8 {{e5}} ... Ke8 {{Kc7}} — the king has cleared a corridor and the pawn is unstoppable.

> Activate the king before pushing the pawn.

steps.prompt: Step the king diagonally forward to **outflank** the defender. Apply the principle: *king first, pawn later*.
steps.hint: Don't touch the pawn yet — clear a lane for it with a diagonal step on the 6th rank.
